document_info.title: Ross Broiler Management Handbook 2025
document_info.purpose: Structured data extraction for AI database training

key_factors_affecting_growth.nutrition: Feed quality, nutrient balance, feed form
key_factors_affecting_growth.temperature: Brooding, house temperature, heat stress management

actions: Clean and disinfect all housing and equipment
Obtain day-old chick information (weight, vaccination, parent stock age, health status)
Preheat house: Air 30°C (86°F) whole-house / 32°C (89.6°F) spot brooding edge
Set up feeders and drinkers, flush water lines
Spread litter 2-4 cm (0.8-1.6 in) depth
Check environmental conditions (temperature, RH, ventilation)
Light intensity: 30-40 lux whole-house / 80-100 lux spot brooding
Monitor chick behavior 1-2 hours after placement
Weigh sample (3 boxes or 1% of population)
Calculate average body weight and flock uniformity (CV%/uniformity%)
Develop appetite from good brooding practices
Adjust environmental conditions based on bird behavior
23 hours light + 1 hour dark, gradually reach 4-6 hours darkness by day 7
Vent temperature: 39.4-40.5°C (103-105°F)
Assess crop fill in first 24 hours
Adjust environmental conditions
Transition to automated feeders
Expand brooding area gradually
Weigh birds individually (minimum 1% or 100 birds)
7-day body weight minimum 4.5x day-old weight
Transition starter to grower feed (10-13 days)
Minimum 4 hours continuous darkness after day 7
Light intensity: 5-10 lux
Bulk weigh at 14 days (minimum 1% or 100 birds)
Adjust drinker and feeder heights
Transition grower to finisher feed (~25 days)
Individual body weights at 21 days
Calculate flock CV%/uniformity%
Weekly individual body weights
Adjust drinker and feeder heights
23 hours light + 1 hour dark for 3 days prior to catching
Feed withdrawal: 8-12 hours before processing
Maintain water access until catching
Ensure catching equipment clean

drinking_space_brooding.space: 8 drinkers per 1,000 chicks (125 chicks per drinker)

feed_form_specifications.particle_size: 3-5 mm (0.12-0.20 in) diameter, 5-7 mm (0.20-0.28 in) length
3-5 mm (0.12-0.20 in) diameter, 6-10 mm (0.24-0.40 in) length

feeding_space_requirements.space: 45-80 birds per pan (lower ratio for >3.5 kg/7.7 lb)

air_quality_standards.notes: Detected by smell at 20+ ppm; >10 damages lungs; >20 increases respiratory disease susceptibility; >25 may reduce growth
>3,500 causes ascites; fatal at high levels
>50 affects health; fatal at high levels
Damages respiratory tract lining; increases disease susceptibility

lighting_program.notes: Lights on at same time each day; comply with local laws
Stabilize feed intake patterns; help keep birds calm

lamp_types.advantages: Good spectral range, can dim, inexpensive
lamp_types.disadvantages: Inefficient, short life (700-1000 hr), high energy cost

lamp_types.advantages: More efficient than incandescent, longer life, reduces electricity cost
lamp_types.disadvantages: Contains mercury, cannot dim, flicker issues, slow warm-up
lamp_types.spectrum: White light 400-700 nm, cool and warm available

lamp_types.advantages: Energy efficient (200 lm/W), long life (50,000 hr), specific colors, some dimmable
lamp_types.disadvantages: High initial cost, cheaper variants unsuitable for poultry houses, potential flicker

lamp_types.advantages: Stable color temperature, almost no light decay, more efficient than incandescent
lamp_types.disadvantages: Not suited to dusty environments, less efficient than LED, emits heat

lamp_types.advantages: Energy efficient, long life, consistent warm color
lamp_types.disadvantages: Sodium hazardous, warm-up time (5-15 min), requires ballast
lamp_types.spectrum: Warm light, highest intensity in yellow/red/orange, ~2100 K

light_wavelength_effects.effect: Promotes calmness, improves FCR, livability, leg health, reduces processing rejects
Increases aggression through increased testosterone production

litter_materials.disadvantages: Often high moisture, mold risk if stored improperly
May increase breast blisters if too wet
Young chicks may eat litter, poor moisture-holding
Tendency to cake, mold risk, pesticide issues

litter_materials.advantages: Can use in arid areas on concrete
litter_materials.disadvantages: Needs good management, difficult cold-weather brooding, ample drying time needed
High caking incidence, mold risk, slow breakdown
Difficult in humid conditions, tendency to cake

stocking_density_regulations.conditions: If exceptionally high welfare standards met over prolonged period

water_quality_criteria.comments: Satisfactory; wet droppings at upper limit
Poor; wet droppings, reduced intake, poor growth, increased mortality
Hard; satisfactory for poultry but interferes with soap/disinfectants
Ideal; levels above indicate fecal contamination

production_efficiency_factor.notes: Higher value = better technical performance; biased by daily gain

feed_conversion_ratio.formula: Total Feed Consumed / Total Live Weight

adjusted_fcr.formula: Actual FCR + (Target Body Weight - Actual Body Weight) / Factor
adjusted_fcr.notes: Useful for comparing at common target weight; avoid beyond ±0.5 lb/227g of actual weight

checks: External building surfaces cleaned and disinfected
External concrete walkways pressure washed with hot water
Grass/vegetation cut back to prevent rodent nesting
Water system cleaned, disinfected, and flushed
Foot baths refreshed with appropriate chemical

pre_catch_checklist.check: Time to catch and transport calculated correctly
Crates/modules and trucks calculated; all equipment clean, disinfected, in good condition
Smooth exit for trucks; ground repaired, compacted, leveled
Wet litter replaced for ease of catching
Removed or repositioned above head height
Reduced; no sudden increase; headlamps or blue light used; curtains over doors if daytime
No heat buildup; sufficient air movement; heaters turned off

investigate: Source flock health and hygiene status
likely_causes: Health and hygiene status of source flock, hatchery, and equipment
Dehydration from excessive hatch spread or late chick removal

investigate: Crop fill in first 2-4 hours post-placement
Availability and accessibility to feed and water
likely_causes: Less than 75-80% crop fill by 2-4 hours
Chicks from wide range of donor flock ages
Chicks unable to find or reach water
Chicks unable to find feed or poor feed quality

troubleshooting_after_7days.issue: Disease (metabolic, bacterial, viral, fungal, protozoal, parasitic, toxins)
High Number of Birds DOA to Processing Plant
investigate: History of feed, water, or power outages

actions: Check hatchery practice, egg handling, and hygiene
Reassess brooding practice
Post-mortems on dead chicks - take veterinary advice
Measure and achieve target crop fill levels
Check feed and water availability and accessibility
Check ventilation rates and feed formulation
Avoid excessive early growth rates
Establish cause via post-mortem
Check water consumption and mineral levels
Check starter ration quality and availability
Check water supply
Reassess brooding practice
Check temperature, humidity, daylength, light uniformity
Ensure downtime >10 days
Seek veterinary advice
Check feed quality and formulation
Avoid excessive early growth restriction
Check ventilation, stocking density, house temperatures
Check water and feed availability
Improve fat quality, avoid excessive salts/protein
Ensure sufficient litter depth, appropriate material
Adjust drinkers, maintain ventilation and humidity control
Consult veterinarian if necessary
Check feeder settings/adjustments
Allow birds to clear feeders once daily from 10-12 days
Check house temperature based on bird behavior
Ensure effective cleaning and disinfection
Clean and disinfect thoroughly between flocks
Reduce dust levels and ensure adequate ventilation

processing_rejects.issue: Skin Lesions (Cellulitis, Dermatitis, Breast Blisters, Hockburn/Pododermatitis)
actions: Maintain appropriate stocking densities and feeding space
Coordinate lights-on with feed availability
Adjust feeder height to encourage feeding but reduce resting at pans
Ensure good feather coverage
Manage litter quality

processing_rejects.characterized_by: Crop/gut with feed or digesta remaining at processing
actions: Adjust feed withdrawal strategy (8-12 hours before processing)
Avoid extended feed withdrawal
Ensure water access until catching
Review light program

processing_rejects.characterized_by: Small undersized birds or loss of muscle
actions: Remove runts/stunts that struggle to access feed/water
Identify and remove emaciated birds during routine checks
Assess bruise color to determine timing
Maintain high welfare standards during handling and catching
Retrain if required
Monitor external catchers

processing_rejects.characterized_by: Green/redder areas at inner breast fillet
actions: Minimize wing flapping during growing period
Catch under low light or blue lights
Assess bruise color to determine timing
Ensure proper crate stocking density for season and distance
Adjust for warmer/colder months
Use temperature-controlled trailers in extreme climates

sight: Bird distribution - areas being avoided suggest environmental issues
Bird respiration - panting indicates temperature/airflow issues
Bird behavior - drinking, feeding, resting split evenly
Bird health - posture, alertness, eyes, gait
Feeders and drinkers - height, feed presence, leaks
Lighting - dark spots, intensity, timer function
hearing: Bird vocalizations - snicking/sneezing, appropriateness for age
Feeder mechanical sounds - augers running constantly, feed bridging
Fan bearings and belts - noise indicates maintenance needed
feel: Air - stuffy/humid, cold, hot, air speed
Feed physical quality - dusty, pellet breakdown
Litter condition - moisture level (should be friable)

bird_assessment.standard: Clear with no signs of irritation
Clean with no feathers sticking out
Hocks clean with no irritation marks
Footpads clean with no irritation markings
Clean with no signs of loose droppings
No nasal discharge, no tongue discoloration or mouth lesions
Feeding, no litter content, not too hard or soft

transport_conditions.standard: Adjusted to ensure chick vent temperature 39.4-40.5°C (103-105°F)

feed_withdrawal.formula: Time in house without feed + Catching time + Transport time + Holding time
factors_affecting_weight_loss: Bird age - higher in older birds

monitoring_indicators.excessive_withdrawal: Watery droppings, watery fluid in small intestine, litter in crop and gizzard
monitoring_indicators.insufficient_withdrawal: Feed in crop, fecal contamination at processing

key_points: Remove whole grains 2 days before processing
Do not disturb flock after feed withdrawal begins
Leave empty feeders down until catching crews arrive

minimum_ventilation.purpose: Provide heat and acceptable air quality, control RH
minimum_ventilation.when_to_use: House temperature at or below set-point, or within 2°C above
key_points: Not all inlets open at same time - uniformly distribute open inlets
Monitor air flow and bird behavior
Heating operates when house temp below set-point even if fans running
Evaluate by bird comfort, behavior, and air quality within 60 seconds of entering

transitional_ventilation.purpose: Temperature control when house temp above set-point but not warm enough for tunnel
key_points: Air directed upward and along ceiling to apex
If birds sitting/huddling, check pressure and inlet airflow, reduce fans if needed
Keep in transitional as long as possible before switching to tunnel

tunnel_ventilation.purpose: Keep birds cool via wind-chill effect
tunnel_ventilation.when_to_use: When birds need cooling effect beyond transitional
tunnel_ventilation.layout: Exhaust fans at one end, air inlets at opposite end
tunnel_ventilation.air_deflectors: First at end of cooling pad, then every 8-9 m (26.2-29.5 ft), minimum 2 m (6.6 ft) above litter
key_points: Switch based on bird behavior, not just temperature
Younger birds feel greater wind chill
Monitor bird behavior: sitting/huddling=cold, wings away from body=too warm, >10% panting=too warm
Measure air speed at 3-4 locations across width, ~30 m from fans

evaporative_cooling.when_to_use: When wind-chill alone no longer keeps birds comfortable
evaporative_cooling.purpose: Hold house temperature at level where birds were last comfortable with all tunnel fans
pad_cooling_management: Control amount of water on pads initially to avoid rapid temperature reduction
Cycle cooling pump on/off for better temperature control
Turn off when house RH exceeds 70%
Hard water reduces cool pad operating life

fogging_systems.notes: Least cooling, greater wet litter risk, not recommended for high RH areas

evaporative_cooling.key_principle: High air speed much more important than evaporative cooling

measures: Isolated area, minimum 3.2 km (2 mi) from nearest poultry/livestock
Away from rivers and ponds to prevent wild bird exposure
Away from major roadways used for poultry transport
Fenced perimeter
Regular water source testing
Minimize traffic flow
Facilitate cleaning and disinfection
Prevent entry by wild birds and rodents
Concrete floors, washable walls and ceilings
Accessible ventilation ducts
No internal pillars or ledges
East-West orientation to minimize solar heat gain
15 m (49.2 ft) cleared area around each house
Lock entry gates, post no trespassing signs
Shower and complete clothing/footwear change for all entering
Visitor records with name, company, purpose, previous/next farms
72 hours downtime after visiting other poultry
Hand and boot sanitization entering/leaving each house
Best practice: change boots between houses
Only necessary cleaned/disinfected tools into house
Visit youngest flocks first
Stop all visits if infectious disease suspected

animal_disease_prevention: Keep vegetation cut 15 m (49.2 ft) from buildings
Ensure feed bins fully closed after deliveries
Store litter in bags or inside storage building/bin
Keep wild birds out - seal all holes/gaps
Continuous rodent baiting program - stations 15-23 m (50-75 ft) apart, max 30 m (100 ft)

live_attenuated.description: Infectious organisms substantially attenuated or modified
live_attenuated.administration: Drinking water, spray, eye drop, or injection
live_attenuated.notes: Most attenuated form given first, followed by less-attenuated if needed

recombinant.description: Live attenuated virus vector carrying desired vaccine antigens
recombinant.notes: Does not carry actual disease virus but produces protective immune response

nipple_drinker_height.notes: Nipples at eye level so chicks can see water droplets
Neck stretching slightly for water, feet remain on ground

bell_drinker_guidelines.at_placement: Minimum 6 bell drinkers (40 cm/15.7 in diameter) per 1,000 chicks
bell_drinker_guidelines.post_brooding: 8 drinkers (40 cm/15.7 in diameter) per 1,000 birds
bell_drinker_guidelines.water_level_7_10_days: 0.6 cm (0.24 in) below top of drinker
bell_drinker_guidelines.water_level_after_10_days: 1 cm (0.39 in) in base of drinker
bell_drinker_guidelines.height_from_day_18: Base level with top of breast
bell_drinker_guidelines.notes: Use ballast to keep level, prevent spillage

feeder_height_guidelines.correct_height: Lip of pan level with top of breast
feeder_height_guidelines.too_high: Increased spillage, economic loss, reduced performance, inaccurate FCR estimates
feeder_height_guidelines.too_low: Birds remain close to feeders, reduced uniformity, increased skin scratches
feeder_height_guidelines.daily_clearing: From days 10-12, allow birds to clear feeders once daily, then refill immediately

sensible_heat_loss.description: Heat loss from warm body to cooler air
sensible_heat_loss.when_dominant: When house temperature at or near set-point, air is cool

latent_heat_loss.when_dominant: When air temperature high, difference between body and air temperature small

house_airtightness_test.procedure: Close all doors and inlets, turn on one 122 cm (48 in)/127 cm (50 in) fan or two 91 cm (36 in) fans
house_airtightness_test.required_pressure: Minimum 3.8 mm (0.15 in) water column (37.5 Pa)
house_airtightness_test.note: Based on house with ~1,850 m² (19,913 ft²) floor area. Smaller areas should achieve higher pressure; larger areas may be less.
